transport.seoDescription: Complete Japan transport guide: Shinkansen bullet trains, JR Pass, domestic flights, buses, metro systems. Routes, prices, and IC card tips.
transport.title: Getting Around Japan
transport.intro: Japan has one of the world's most efficient and extensive public transportation systems. From the legendary Shinkansen bullet trains to comprehensive urban metro networks, getting around Japan is a seamless experience.
transport.quickComparison: Quick Cost Comparison: Tokyo to Osaka
transport.shinkansenIntro: Japan's Shinkansen network is the gold standard of high-speed rail, connecting major cities at speeds up to 320 km/h. Clean, punctual (average delay: 36 seconds), and comfortable.
transport.jrPassIntro: The JR Pass offers unlimited travel on most JR trains including Shinkansen. Available for 7, 14, or 21 days.
transport.domesticFlightsIntro: For longer distances (Tokyo to Sapporo or Fukuoka), domestic flights can be faster and sometimes cheaper than Shinkansen.
transport.metroDesc: Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, Nagoya, Fukuoka, and Sapporo all have excellent subway systems. Buy an IC card (Suica or PASMO) for seamless payment.
transport.icCardsDesc: Rechargeable transit cards that work on virtually all trains, buses, and even convenience stores across Japan. Buy at any station kiosk.
transport.taxiDesc: Clean and safe but expensive. Starting fare around ¥500-700 ($3.50-4.70). Use for short distances or late-night travel.
transport.busDesc: Comprehensive bus networks in all cities. Pay with IC card or exact change. Kyoto's bus system is particularly useful for temple-hopping.
transport.bookingTipText: Buy your Suica/PASMO card at any JR station kiosk. It costs ¥500 deposit + your desired balance. You can use it nationwide on trains, buses, and for purchases at convenience stores and vending machines.

visa.seoDescription: Complete Japan visa guide: visa-free countries, eVisa process, Visit Japan Web registration, required documents, and entry rules for 2026.
visa.title: Japan Visa Guide 2026
visa.intro: Japan offers visa-free entry to citizens of 68+ countries for short tourist stays. Here is everything you need to know about entering Japan.
visa.visaFreeDesc: Citizens of many countries can enter Japan without a visa for stays of 90 days (or 15-30 days for some nationalities). This includes most EU countries, USA, Canada, Australia, UK, and many Asian countries.
visa.evisaDesc: Japan is expanding its eVisa system. Citizens of eligible countries can apply online through the Japan eVisa portal. Processing typically takes 5-7 business days.
visa.visitJapanWebDesc: All visitors must register on Visit Japan Web before arrival. This digital platform handles immigration, customs, and tax-free shopping registration.
visa.requiredDocsDesc: Valid passport (6+ months validity), return/onward ticket, proof of accommodation, proof of sufficient funds.
visa.faq1Q: How long can I stay in Japan visa-free?
visa.faq1A: Most visa-free travelers can stay 90 days. Some nationalities are limited to 15 or 30 days. Check with your local Japanese embassy for specifics.
visa.faq2Q: Do I need to register on Visit Japan Web?
visa.faq2A: Yes, all visitors should register on Visit Japan Web before arrival. It speeds up immigration processing and is required for tax-free shopping.
visa.faq3Q: Can I extend my tourist visa?
visa.faq3A: Extensions are possible at local immigration offices but not guaranteed. You'll need a valid reason and supporting documents.

weather.seoTitle: Japan Weather Guide - Best Time to Visit by Season
weather.seoDescription: Month-by-month Japan weather guide with temperatures, rainfall, and best activities. Plan your trip around cherry blossoms, autumn foliage, or winter skiing.
weather.title: Japan Weather Guide
weather.intro: Japan experiences four distinct seasons, each offering unique experiences. From cherry blossoms in spring to powder snow in winter, timing your visit can make all the difference.
weather.springDesc: Cherry blossom season! Mild temperatures (10-20°C), occasional rain. The most popular time to visit Japan.
weather.summerDesc: Hot and humid (25-35°C). Rainy season (tsuyu) in June-July. Festival season with fireworks and matsuri.
weather.autumnDesc: Beautiful fall foliage (koyo). Comfortable temperatures (15-25°C). Less crowded than spring.
weather.winterDesc: Cold (0-10°C) with heavy snow in northern Japan. Perfect for skiing, onsen, and winter illuminations.

practicalInfo.seoTitle: Japan Practical Travel Info - Essential Tips
practicalInfo.seoDescription: Essential practical information for traveling in Japan: money, language, etiquette, connectivity, safety, and cultural tips for first-time visitors.
practicalInfo.title: Practical Travel Info for Japan
practicalInfo.intro: Japan is one of the safest and most organized countries to travel in, but understanding local customs and practical details will enhance your experience.
practicalInfo.moneyDesc: Japan uses the Japanese Yen (JPY/¥). While cashless payment is growing, Japan remains more cash-dependent than many countries. Always carry cash, especially outside major cities.
practicalInfo.atm: ATMs: 7-Eleven and Japan Post ATMs accept international cards 24/7
practicalInfo.creditCards: Credit cards accepted at hotels and larger restaurants, but many small shops are cash-only
practicalInfo.languageDesc: Japanese is the official language. English proficiency is limited outside tourist areas, but signs in train stations and airports have English translations. Download Google Translate with offline Japanese pack.
practicalInfo.etiquetteDesc: Remove shoes when entering homes, temples, and some restaurants. Bow when greeting. Don't tip (it can be considered rude). Be quiet on trains. Don't eat while walking.
practicalInfo.connectivityDesc: Japan has excellent 4G/5G coverage. Options: pocket WiFi rental, prepaid SIM cards, or eSIM. Free WiFi available at stations, convenience stores, and many cafes.
practicalInfo.safetyDesc: Japan is one of the safest countries in the world. Crime rates are extremely low. Lost items are often returned. Emergency number: 110 (police) or 119 (ambulance/fire).
practicalInfo.faq1Q: Do I need to tip in Japan?
practicalInfo.faq1A: No. Tipping is not customary in Japan and can sometimes be considered rude. Service charges are included in prices.
practicalInfo.faq2A: Japan can be surprisingly affordable. Budget travelers can manage on $50-80/day with konbini meals, hostels, and public transport. Mid-range travelers should budget $100-200/day.
practicalInfo.faq3Q: Do I need to speak Japanese?
practicalInfo.faq3A: Not necessarily, but learning basic phrases (arigatou, sumimasen, kudasai) is appreciated. Google Translate's camera feature works well for menus and signs.

esim.seoTitle: Japan eSIM & Internet Guide - Stay Connected
esim.seoDescription: Best ways to get internet in Japan: eSIM, pocket WiFi, SIM cards. Compare options, prices, and coverage for travelers.
esim.title: Japan eSIM & Internet Guide
esim.intro: Staying connected in Japan is essential for navigation, translation, and communication. Here are your best options for mobile internet.
esim.esimDesc: The easiest option for modern phones. Buy before you travel, activate on arrival. No physical SIM swap needed.
esim.pocketWifiDesc: Rent a portable WiFi device. Great for groups or if your phone doesn't support eSIM. Pick up at airports.
esim.simCardDesc: Available at airports and electronics stores. Typically data-only (no phone calls). IIJmio and Mobal are popular options.

insurance.seoDescription: Why you need travel insurance for Japan and how to choose the right policy. Medical costs, coverage tips, and recommended providers.
insurance.title: Travel Insurance for Japan
insurance.intro: While Japan is very safe, medical costs for foreigners can be extremely high. Travel insurance is strongly recommended.
insurance.whyDesc: Japanese hospitals can charge foreigners full rates. A simple hospital visit can cost ¥30,000-50,000 ($200-330). Surgery or extended stays can run into tens of thousands of dollars.
insurance.adventure: Adventure sports coverage (if skiing or hiking)
